coredata.dc:title: AgNPs/QDs@GQDs nanocomposites developed as an ultrasensitive impedimetric aptasensor for ractopamine detection
coredata.dc:creator: Roushani, Mahmoud
Ghanbarzadeh, Mahsa
Shahdost-fard, Faezeh
Sahraei, Reza
Soheyli, Ehsan
coredata.dc:description: Developing easy-to-use and miniaturized sensors for in-field monitoring of targets which is related to human health is necessary. Ractopamine (RAC) is a feed additive with serious side effects that is forbidden in many countries. This study reports the fabrication of an impedimetric aptasensor for ultrasensitive and selective detection of the RAC in human biological fluids. Accordingly, an efficient nanocomposites was synthesized by a beneficial combination of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), quantum dots (QDs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) for modifying a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). This nanocomposite is promising to present a synergistic effect in the increase of the active surface area of the modified electrode to more load the biocapture of the target. Next, the RAC-binding aptamer (Apt) was attached to the AgNPs/QDs@GQDs/GCE surface and a sensitive layer for the RAC detection was embedded. A RAC-Apt complex was formed upon adding the RAC and the changes of the electrochemical behavior were studied by some electrochemical techniques such as electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Under optimal conditions, the charge transfer resistance (Rct) value was increased linearly with increasing of the RAC concentrations in the range of 1 fM to 901.4 nM. Limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 330 aM which is superior by other reported electrochemical methods in the RAC sensing. The applicability of the aptasensor was tested in human urine and blood serum as the real samples and satisfactory results of specificity were achieved. It seems that the proposed strategy not only provides a new ultrasensitive strategy for RAC detection but also expands the application of the sensing interface to develop other aptasensors by changing the Apt sequence.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Impedimetric aptasensor
Ractopamine
Graphene quantum dots
Cu-In-S/ZnS quantum dots
Silver nanoparticles
Nanocomposites

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Principle of the preparation of the AgNPs/QDs@GQDs/GCE as the designed aptasensor
Characterization of the synthesized AgNPs/QDs@GQDs nanocomposites
Morphology scrutiny of the modified electrodes in steps of the QDs@GQDs/GCE and Apt/AgNPs/QDs@GQDs/GCE
Study of the characterization of the sensing interface
Investigation of the performance of the RAC aptasensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the supports from Ilam University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of the bare GCE (a) QDs@GQDs/GCE (b) and Apt/AgNPs/QDs@GQDs (c).

(A) The recorded Nyquist curves and (B) CVs of the different modified electrodes in each step of the modification process: (a) GCE, (b) QDs@GQDs/GCE, (c) AgNPs/QDs@GQDs/GCE, (d) Apt/AgNPs/QDs@GQDs/GCE, (e) Apt/AgNPs/QDs@GQDs/GCE after incubation with 600 fM RAC in a solution containing 5 mM [Fe(CN6)]3 − /4 − (at a ratio of 1:1) and 0.1 M KCl as the redox probe with scan rate of 100 mV s− 1.
Effect of incubation time on the developed aptasensor response for 900 pM RAC. 60 min is optimum time.

The plots of the impedimetric response of the aptasensor against different concentrations of the RAC in three linear ranges (low concentration: 1 fM to 1.1 pM, middle concentration: 1.1 pM to 1.4 nM and high concentration 1.4 nM to 901.4 nM).

The recorded Nyquist curves after incubation of 0.3 pM of some probable interfering species such as (A) NaCl, (B), L-Arg, (C) Gly, (D) Ascorbic acid, (E) Trp, (F) Pyl, (G) Tyr and (H) 1 fM RAC. (I) Histogram of the EIS response changes of the proposed aptasensor for RAC as the target molecule against the interfering species.
(A) The RAC molecular structure and (B) the schematic of fabrication of developed impedimetric aptasensor for the RAC detection.
A comparison of the characteristics of different sensors for RAC detection.

row.entry: Ab/Agpd NPs d /BSA/artificial antigen/rGO e /SPCE f

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: RAC detection in human urine and blood serum samples with the designed aptasensor (means of three different determinations).

head.ce:title: AgNPs/QDs@GQDs nanocomposites developed as an ultrasensitive impedimetric aptasensor for ractopamine detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ilam University, 65315-516 Ilam, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ilam University, 65315-516, Ilam, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, Ilam University, 65315-516 Ilam, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, Ilam University, 65315-516, Ilam, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing easy-to-use and miniaturized sensors for in-field monitoring of targets which is related to human health is necessary. Ractopamine (RAC) is a feed additive with serious side effects that is forbidden in many countries. This study reports the fabrication of an impedimetric aptasensor for ultrasensitive and selective detection of the RAC in human biological fluids. Accordingly, an efficient nanocomposites was synthesized by a beneficial combination of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), quantum dots (QDs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) for modifying a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). This nanocomposite is promising to present a synergistic effect in the increase of the active surface area of the modified electrode to more load the biocapture of the target. Next, the RAC-binding aptamer (Apt) was attached to the AgNPs/QDs@GQDs/GCE surface and a sensitive layer for the RAC detection was embedded. A RAC-Apt complex was formed upon adding the RAC and the changes of the electrochemical behavior were studied by some electrochemical techniques such as electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Under optimal conditions, the charge transfer resistance (Rct) value was increased linearly with increasing of the RAC concentrations in the range of 1 fM to 901.4 nM. Limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 330 aM which is superior by other reported electrochemical methods in the RAC sensing. The applicability of the aptasensor was tested in human urine and blood serum as the real samples and satisfactory results of specificity were achieved. It seems that the proposed strategy not only provides a new ultrasensitive strategy for RAC detection but also expands the application of the sensing interface to develop other aptasensors by changing the Apt sequence.

ce:section.ce:para: Ractopamine (RAC), C18H23NO3, is a β-adrenergic agonist which is originally employed to treat respiratory diseases related to the lung and asthma (Scheme 1A) [1]. On the other hand, this is illegally employed in animal feeding to enhance muscle content and decreasing adipose tissue deposition to increase growth rate [2]. In most countries, this feed additive is rigorously banned based on the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) regulations. This mimics the effects of adrenaline and results in an increase of the protein synthesis in muscle tissue during the administration period. As a result, the accumulated RAC in animal tissues lead to induce potential risks for consumer health with serious side effects such as muscle tremors, tachycardia, and headache [3]. So, distinguish of the RAC in the human biological fluids in the viewpoint of the judicial authorities and forensic medicine is so important [4–6]. Up to now, various analytical methods have been reported for the detection of the RAC including gas chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (GS-MS/MS) [7], High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8], enzyme-linked immunoassay [9], and capillary electrophoresis [10]. Thus, although a significant improvement has been made by the mentioned methods, a portable and reliable method for the sensitive and selective measurement of the RAC still faces challenges [11].

Recently, among the different electrochemical sensors, the aptamer (Apt)-based electrochemical sensors, aptasensors, widely employed in various applications due to unique advantages to use as a miniaturized and portable device [12–14]. The performance of these aptasensors is seriously affected by the character of the sensing interface. Several nanocomposites have been used in the design of various electrochemical aptasensors including reduced graphene oxide/nile blue/gold nanoparticles (rGO/NB/AuNPs) [15], carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) [16], Ketjen black/ferrocene dual-doped MOFs and aptamer-coupling AuNPs (AuNPs/Fc-KB/ZIF-8) [17], rGO/silver nanoparticles (rGO/AgNPs) [18], copper nanoparticles electrodeposited on rGO-based hybrids (CuNPs/PDA-rGO-NB) [19] and bimetallic nanoparticle/graphene oxide/thionine composite (Au-AgNPs/GO/TH) [20]. Among various nanomaterials, quantum dots (QDs) as an emerging class of fluorophores have been qualified by their fashion characteristics especially those related to structure and sizes. The mutual presence of quantum confinement effects and very large surface-to-volume ratio makes QDs favorable for extensive utilization in a wide variety of manners including as emitters in optoelectronic devices [21] and as basic elements for chemical and biological sensing [22–24]. However, free of heavy metal ions, hydrophilic indium-based QDs are the newest type of such luminescent structures for various applications in biology and medicine [25]. The most striking property of the aqueous medium for preparing these class of the nanomaterials is the lack of necessity to change the dispersion media for biological intentions, which preserves the surface properties of as-prepared QDs. Cu-In-S (CIS) nanocrystals are one of this QDs category with high molar absorptivity, long exciton lifetime, and promising charge transfer properties [26]. However, bare CIS QDs are not usually stable in air or water. In this regard, creating core/shell heterostructures of the QDs by coating the CIS cores with layers of a ZnS shell (CIS/ZnS QDs) is a well-accepted strategy to enhance stability, while at the same time fulfills the biological advantages [27]. Thus, this promising nanomaterials regard to the various functional groups and biological advantages of them have attractive benefits in bioelectrochemical applications. On the other hand, utilizing another nanomaterial such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in combination with the QDs may present synergistic effects on their unique properties in electrochemical sensing [28]. Due to the GQDs offer significant advantages including small sizes, abundant edge sites, low toxicity and high electrical conductivity via electron transfer tunneling, they have been used as a functional component in the fabrication of specific devices [29–32]. According to the literature, the clever combination of the CIS/ZnS QDs and GQDs for the fabrication of admirable nanocomposites in constructing an aptasensor has not yet been investigated. To do so, in the present study, the GQDs is synthesized and the CIS/ZnS QDs as a layer is covered on its surface (CIS/ZnS QDs@GQDs). Next, by utilizing the beneficial advantages of the silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) and the synthesized CIS/ZnS QDs@GQDs, the AgNPs/QDs@GQDs nanocomposites is embedded. In the following, the Apt is bonded onto the nanocomposites to construct the RAC aptasensor. It is noteworthy that the AgNPs acts as a linker to attach onto the QDs@GQDs surface via AgNP-S bonds from one side and more load the Apt sequence via NH2-AgNPs bonds from another side. Finally, the applicability of the nanocomposites is evaluated in preparation of the aptasensor in the ultrasensitive detection of the RAC target in the spiked urine and blood serum samples.

The functional sequence of the RAC Apt labeled with NH2 group containing 15 bases (5′-NH2-AAAAGTGCGGGCAAA-3′) [33] was ordered from Bioneer Company (South Korea). Glycine (Gly), Ascorbic acid (AA), NaCl, L-Arginine (L-Arg), tyrosine (Tyr), tryptophan (Trp) and pyrrolysine (Pyl) were used for investigation of the selectivity effect. The RAC, sodium borohydride (NaBH4), silver nitrate (AgNO3), trisodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O), Zn(OAc)2·2H2O, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, In(NO3)3, N-acetyl-L-cysteine (NAC), Na3C6H5O7 (trisodium citrate dehydrate), Na2S·H2O, CH4N2S (thiourea, TU), CH3COCH3 (acetone), citric acid (CA) and all other reagents were purchased from Merck, Fluka or Sigma-Aldrich Companies. All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

Precursor solutions of 0.01 M copper nitrate, 1.0 M sodium citrate (SC), and 1.0 M Na2S were prepared in deionized water and a precursor solution of 1 M indium nitrate was prepared in ethanol The ZnS stock solution was also obtained by adding 0.18 g zinc acetate, 0.2 g NAC, and 0.06 g TU to 20 mL deionized water. Furthermore, phosphate buffer (PB, 0.1 M, pH 7.4) was prepared with a stock standard solution of Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4. A solution containing 5.0 mM K3Fe(CN6)/K4Fe(CN6) (1:1) and 0.1 M KCl was used in all of the electrochemical experiments as a redox probe. A human urine sample was collected from a healthy human. Also, a healthy blood serum sample was obtained from a local hospital. In order to determine the RAC, different concentrations of the RAC were prepared by dilution of a stock solution of the RAC. All experiments were carried out at room temperature (25 °C ± 0.1).

All electrochemical measurements were carried out in a three-electrode cell by using a μ-AUTOLAB electrochemical system Potentiostat/Galvanostat (Eco-Chemie, Switzerland) driven with NOVA 2.1 software. The three electrode cell was composed of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE), an Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) and a Pt wire as the working, reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The elemental content of the samples was determined by an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS) model Oxford INCA II energy solid state detector. Infrared transmittance spectra of the samples were obtained on a Bruker Vertex 70 FT-IR spectrometer in the mid-infrared region (400–4000 cm−1). KBr was used for pelleting of QDs powder with 0.5 cm−1 resolution. Philips CM30 electron microscope at an operating voltage of 300 kV was applied for TEM analysis of the samples. The images of the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) were recorded using a SEM apparatus (Philip's Company, Netherlands). The optical absorption spectrum of the sample was measured by Cary 300 Bio UV–vis spectrophotometer (VARIAN). Photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were recorded at different excitation and emission wavelengths using Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent technology).
This section is presented in Electronic Supplementary Materials.

The required amounts of the precursor solutions containing 8.0 mg NAC, 75 μL indium nitrate, 0.4 mL SC and 0.9 mL copper nitrate were mixed in 40 mL deionized water, followed by regulating pH at 8.0 (by 1 M NaOH). Then, the obtained solution was loaded into a three-necked flask under a very weak nitrogen atmosphere and stirred for 15 min. Next, 0.065 mL of Na2S precursor was quickly injected into the stirred mixture at room temperature. After that, the mixture was heated to 95 °C and maintained at this temperature for 45 min (after this duration, aliquots were excluded from the solution as CIS QDs). 5.5 mL of the ZnS stock solution was swiftly added to the vigorously stirred solution and kept refluxing for an extra 45 min to form CIS/ZnS core-shell QDs. The as-synthesized colloidal solution was allowed to cool down to room temperature. Optical measurements were conducted at this step. The reaction solution was precipitated with an excess amount of acetone followed by centrifugation at 4000 rpm, decantation, and washing by deionized water for several times. The CIS/ZnS QDs was used for further experiments.

1.0 mL of the synthesized CIS/ZnS QDs was added into 5.0 mL the obtained GQDs suspension and stirred for 2 h to be properly mixed. The mixture of the QDs and GQDs was then dried at 50 °C overnight in an oven. The obtained powder was then annealed at 150 °C for an hour. In this way, a layer of the CIS/ZnS QDs was covered onto the GQDs surface. The obtained CIS/ZnS QDs@GQDs which is denoted as the QDs@GQDs was stored in 4 °C for further experiments.
The AgNPs was synthesized according to our previous studies [35]. (More details are presented in Electronic Supplementary Materials).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of the preparation of the AgNPs/QDs@GQDs/GCE as the designed aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of the aptasensor is shown in Scheme 1B. Firstly, a GCE surface was carefully polished with 0.03 μm alumina powder on polishing cloth. Then, the polished GCE was sonicated in a mixture of deionized water and absolute ethanol (1:1) for 5 min to remove the absorbed particles. After the cleaned GCE was dried at room temperature, the GCE surface was coated by 10 μL of the synthesized QDs@GQDs and allowed to dry in the air (for 1 h) to achieve the QDs@GQDs/GCE. Subsequently, 10 μL of the AgNPs was dropped onto the QDs@GQDs/GCE surface (for 4 h) to attach on the thiol groups of the QDs on the QDs@GQDs/GCE surface. Finally, 10 μL of the NH2-Apt was incubated onto the surface of the AgNPs/QDs@GQDs/GCE to construct the Apt/AgNPs/QDs@GQDs/GCE as the aptasensor. Consequently, the achieved aptasensor was employed for RAC detection via electrochemical experiments in the following investigation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the synthesized AgNPs/QDs@GQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the successful synthesize of the GQDs, the FT-IR technique was applied. Furthermore, UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of the GQDs were checked. To further confirming of the GQDs with nanosize structure, TEM technique was used (More details are presented in Electronic Supplementary Materials). The QDs was characterized using EDS, FT-IR and TEM techniques. The EDS and FT-IR measurements gave the chemical composition and interaction of stabilizing molecules with QDs in these particles, respectively. Furthermore, TEM image was taken to determine the size and shape of the QDs. Also, the UV–vis absorption, PLE and PL spectra of the dual-capped QDs at different emission and excitation wavelengths were investigated (More details are presented in Electronic Supplementary Materials). According to the results of the previous studies [28], the presence of the AgNPs as a linker is essential to the preparation of the nanocomposites to attach the Apt sequence onto the electrode surface. To confirm the characterization of the prepared AgNPs, TEM technique was applied. Moreover, the UV–vis technique was employed in the range of 300–700 nm as another witness for checking of the synthesized AgNPs (More details are presented in Electronic Supplementary Materials).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology scrutiny of the modified electrodes in steps of the QDs@GQDs/GCE and Apt/AgNPs/QDs@GQDs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of different modified electrodes in each step was characterized by the SEM technique. As shown in Fig. 1b, it can be concluded that a nanostructure layer of the QDs@GQDs is successfully immobilized on the bare GCE surface (Fig. 1a) and the Apt/AgNPs/QDs@GQDs/GCE as a sensing interface is achieved (Fig. 1c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the characterization of the sensing interface
ce:section.ce:para: The RAC aptasensor fabrication process was verified by an investigation of the changes in the electrochemical behavior of the modified electrode in each step of the electrode modification process. Fig. 2A shows the Nyquist curves related to the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of different electrodes at a potential of 0.23 V in the redox probe. The change of the diameter of the semicircle as a reflecting parameter of the charge transfer resistance, Rct, is a key factor to characterize of kinetic of the probe at the electrode interface in this technique. By modification of the electrode surface in the first step, the Rct value was increased for the QDs@GQDs/GCE (Rct = 790 Ω) in compared to the bare GCE (Rct = 98 Ω). This increase of Rct value can be referred to repulsion between the negative partial charges of the QDs@GQDs with the anion redox probe. By coating of the AgNPs and formation of the AgNPs/QDs@GQDs nanocomposites onto the electrode surface, Rct value was increased to 1462 Ω. This electrochemical behavior can be attributed to the attachment of the citrate-capped AgNPs with negative charge in the nanocomposites which is immobilized onto the thiol groups of the QDs@GQDs via the bonding formation of the AgS. So, bridges related to the electron transfer from the redox probe onto the modified electrode surface was decreased. Immobilization of the Apt on the surface of the AgNPs/QDs@GQDs/GCE in next step led to dramatically increase of the Rct value to 2784 Ω. This significant decrease of the electron transfer ability reflects the Apt sequence with negative insulating nature is attached to the AgNPs/QDs@GQDs/GCE surface via chemisorption bonding of the AgNPs in the nanocomposites and NH2 groups in the 5′-terminal of the Apt sequence. By incubation of 600 fM RAC as the target for 60 min on the prepared aptasensor surface, the diameter of the semicircle significantly was increased (Rct = 6480 Ω). This is strong proof that the RAC and Apt by the formation of a RAC/Apt complex obviously prevent the electron transfer of the redox probe in the electrode surface. According to these results, the achieved EIS results corroborate the successful construction of the aptasensor. To more study the characterization of the sensing interface, cyclic voltammetry (CV) technique was applied as another proof for confirming the layer by layer immobilization of each substrate in the modified electrode (Fig. 2B). Firstly, a sharp CV related to reversible redox response of the bare GCE in the redox probe of the ([Fe(CN6)]3 − /4 −) with a peak potential separation (∆E) of 90 mV was observed. Secondly, after dropping the QDs@GQDs on the surface of the GCE, the peak current obviously decreased and the ∆E value was increased to 150 mV. This behavior attributes to repel between the negative charge of the QDs@GQDs and anion probe. Thirdly, the immobilization of the AgNPs onto the thiol groups of the QDs@QGDs, which forms a self-assembled monolayer via Ag-s bonds onto the surface of the QDs@GQDs/GCE, decreases current signal and increases ∆E value (∆E = 188 mV). Next, the presence of the Apt sequence which was grafted on the modified GCE surface formed a cluster of long spacer arms on the platform resulting in the more blocking of the electron flow of the probe to the electrode surface (∆E = 225 mV). Finally, with incubation of the RAC onto the aptasensor surface, the peak current was decreased and ∆E value was increased to 357 mV. Consequently, all results present the obtained data from the CV technique are in good agreement with the achieved results from the EIS technique and both of them confirm that the sensing interface is successfully achieved.

To achieve an optimal impedimetric response, the optimization of experimental conditions for the aptasensor fabrication is essential. To study the binding time of the RAC with the Apt, the aptasensor was separately incubated in the 900 pM RAC for different times of 15, 30, 45, 60, 75, 90 and 105 min (Fig. 3 ). According to the results, the ∆Rct value increased by the increase of binding time to 60 min and tended to a strong decrease after 60 min. This behavior may be due to the conformation change of the attached Apt onto the modified electrode surface upon RAC recognition and the subsequent steric/conformational restrictions. So, 60 min as the incubation time was used in the following experiment which is according to the literature [33]. Based on our previous studies, pH value of the redox probe and other solutions was adjusted in 7.4 [12,40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the performance of the RAC aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of each sensor is a very essential parameter in the evaluation of the performance of the measuring method. To investigate this parameter, the aptasensor was incubated in the presence of seven possible interfering species toward RAC detection. L-Arg, AA, Gly, NaCl, Tyr, Trp and Pyl were used and the related Nyquist plots were recorded. The related Nyquist curves in Fig. 5 indicate dropping of 1 fM RAC onto the aptasensor surface causes a discernable change in Rct value while incubating 0.3 pM of the species (300 fold higher than that of the RAC) reflect less or no change in the Rct value. Therefore, the aptasensor is highly specific for the RAC measurement. The plotted histogram of this study is presented in Fig. 5I.

Furthermore, to examine the stability parameter as another important factor for evaluation of each sensor, the CVs of the aptasensor in electrolyte solution were recorded. The results showed that in one continuous operation, 95% of the original response toward 300 nM of the RAC remained after 50 repetitive cycles and nearly no significant change in peak potential separation was observed. This behavior reflects the satisfactory stability of the constructed aptasensor. Furthermore, to test the reproducibility of the aptasensor, four modified GCEs for the detection of 300 nM RAC were independently prepared and checked by the EIS technique. Based on the obtained data, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 4.7% was calculated indicating that the aptasensor results are reproducible. Additionally, the storage stability of the aptasensor was studied by incubation of 300 nM RAC onto the aptasensor surface and keeping at 4 °C during 10 days. The results of the CV technique presented 96% of its initial response was preserved after this time reflecting good stability of the aptasensor.

In order to evaluation of the efficiency of the aptasensor for practical applications, human urine sample which was prepared from a 24 years old healthy girl was examined. To do so, 1 mL of the urine with 4 mL PB (pH = 7.4, 0.1 M) at a ratio of 1:4 was diluted and different concentrations of the RAC (1.01 × 10− 1 pM, 3.01 × 102 pM and 1.00 × 105 pM) in spiked urine samples were prepared. In addition, the proposed aptasensor was also tested for the detection of the RAC in normal blood serum specimen. The blood serum was diluted 3 times with PB (pH = 7.4, 0.1 M) and the different spiked concentrations of the RAC (1.01 × 10− 1 pM, 3.01 × 102 pM and 1.00 × 105 pM) in the blood serum samples were analyzed by the aptasensor. For analysis of the urine and blood serum samples, the standard addition method and EIS technique were applied. As listed in Table 2 , the values of the recovery, standard deviation (SD) and RSD for different concentrations of the RAC in the spiked samples are calculated. It can be concluded that the proposed strategy is promising to detect the RAC in the human biological fluids with sufficient and good accuracy.

To sum up, an efficient impedimetric aptasensor for the ultrasensitive and selective detection of the RAC using AgNPs/QDs@GQDs nanocomposites was fabricated. Under optimized conditions and using EIS as a measuring technique, the RAC detection was achieved with the lowest LOD value of the detection compared to other reported methods with a concentration range up to 901.4 nM. The results showed the aptasensor has a capability to successfully employ for the RAC determination in the complex matrixes such as human urine and blood serum samples. The reasons behind these satisfactory results are mostly attributed to the unique features of the prepared nanocomposites. The presence of the thiol group in the QDs@GQDs nanolayer led to more capturing of the AgNPs onto the nanolayer by self-assembly of these nanoparticles via thiol-AgNPs bonds. Also, more attachment of the AgNPs as a linker on the nanocomposites surface made more active sites for more loading of the Apt sequence onto the modified GCE surface. In this way, more RAC-binding Apt was immobilized onto the prepared surface to capture more RAC molecule on its arm and detect the RAC with a trace level. Importantly, the GQDs and AgNPs were synthesized from cost-effective primary substrates with a simple method which is so beneficial parameter in sensor designing. The proposed strategy is promising to have potential in a competitive approach for sensitive sensing of the RAC in human biological fluids in the real world.

Mahmoud Roushani: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Mahsa Ghanbarzadeh: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Software, Validation, Visualization.Faezeh Shahdost-fard: Investigation. Reza Sahraei: Formal analysis. Ehsan Soheyli: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have no affiliation with any organization with a direct or indirect financial interest in the subject matter discussed in the manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the supports from Ilam University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2019.110507.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An electrochemical immunosensor based on pristine graphene for rapid determination of ractopamine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Jiang, S. Qi, B. Zhao, An electrochemical immunosensor based on pristine graphene for rapid determination of ractopamine, Chem Phys Lett. 685 (2017) 146-150.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The effect of ractopamine hydrochloride (Paylean®) on lean carcass yields and pork quality characteristics of heavy pigs fed normal and amino acid fortified diets

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.N. Carr, D.N. Hamilton, K.D. Miller, A.L. Schroeder, D. Fernández-Dueñas, J. Killefer, F.K. McKeith, The effect of ractopamine hydrochloride (Paylean®) on lean carcass yields and pork quality characteristics of heavy pigs fed normal and amino acid fortified diets, Meat Sci. 81 (2009) 533-539.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced simultaneous detection of ractopamine and salbutamol via electrochemical-facial deposition of Mno2 nanoflowers onto 3D RGO/Ni foam templates
